coredata.dc:title: Synergetic tuning of photocatalytic activity and photostability of Ag3PO4 via yttrium doping, carbon quantum dots and BiVO4 for atenolol degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yifei
Niu, Jing
Gao, Xin
Zhang, Yanlin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Y-doped Ag3PO4, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), BiVO4 were used to synthesize Z-scheme Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4. X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were employed to probe into the crystalline structure, morphology, optical property and chemical composition of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4. The kinetic constant of the prepared Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was approximately 2.8 times that of pristine Ag3PO4 for atenolol degradation. The degradation of atenolol mainly depended on the excited holes, •OH and •O2 −. The photocatalysis mechanism of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 conforms to the Z-scheme mechanism deduced by reactive species trapping experiment, electron spin resonance (ESR) and photoelectrochemical measurement (photocurrent response and EIS). The introduction of yttrium, CQDs and BiVO4 significantly promoted the photogenerated carriers separation of Ag3PO4 and inhibited the photogenerated electron-holes pairs recombination of Ag3PO4 during the degradation of atenolol. Oxygen defects caused by the doping of Y3+ into Ag3PO4 were the centers of capture of photogenerated electrons to generate •O2 −, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-holes pairs and the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver orthophosphate
Atenolol degradation
Reactive species
Synergetic effect
Oxygen defect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ag3PO4 and Y3+-doped Ag3PO4
Fabrication of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs, Y-Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of prepared photocatalysts (a) before degradation, and (b) after degradation.
SEM images of (a) Y-Ag3PO4, (b) BiVO4, (c) Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 and TEM images of (d) CQDs, (e) Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs, (f) Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4.

The XPS spectra of (a) survey spectra, (d) O1s, (f) C1s of Y-Ag3PO4, BiVO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4, (b) Ag3d, (c) P2p, (e) Y3d spectra of Y-Ag3PO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4, and (d) V2p, (e) Bi4f spectra of BiVO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4, respectively.
FT-IR spectra of Ag3PO4, Y-Ag3PO4, BiVO4, Y-Ag3PO4/BiVO4, Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4.
(a) UV–vis DRS of prepared samples, and (b) plots of (αhν)2 versus energy (hν) of Ag3PO4, Y-Ag3PO4, BiVO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4.
The PL spectra of the as-prepared photocatalysts.

Photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts for atenolol degradation (a), corresponding kinetic plots for the photocatalytic degradation of atenolol (b), cycling tests of Y-Ag3PO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 for the degradation of atenolol (c).
Plots of reactive species trapping experiment of atenolol degradation by Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4.
ESR spectra of (a) DMPO-•OH, (b) DMPO-•O2 − adducts of Y-Ag3PO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 before and after visible light irradiation.
Possible degradation pathways of atenolol by Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4.
TOC removal during the atenolol degradation by Y-Ag3PO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4.
(a) Photocurrent-t response, and (b) EIS Nyquist plots of prepared photocatalysts.
Z-scheme photocatalysis mechanism for atenolol degradation by Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4.

head.ce:title: Synergetic tuning of photocatalytic activity and photostability of Ag3PO4 via yttrium doping, carbon quantum dots and BiVO4 for atenolol degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution and Environmental Safety & Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment Ministry of Education, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution and Environmental Safety & Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution and Environmental Safety & Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment Ministry of Education, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Jiarong Environmental Protection & New Energy Co., Guangzhou 510725, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Jiarong Environmental Protection & New Energy Co.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Jiarong Environmental Protection & New Energy Co., Guangzhou 510725, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Y-doped Ag3PO4, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), BiVO4 were used to synthesize Z-scheme Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4. X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were employed to probe into the crystalline structure, morphology, optical property and chemical composition of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4. The kinetic constant of the prepared Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was approximately 2.8 times that of pristine Ag3PO4 for atenolol degradation. The degradation of atenolol mainly depended on the excited holes, •OH and •O2 −. The photocatalysis mechanism of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 conforms to the Z-scheme mechanism deduced by reactive species trapping experiment, electron spin resonance (ESR) and photoelectrochemical measurement (photocurrent response and EIS). The introduction of yttrium, CQDs and BiVO4 significantly promoted the photogenerated carriers separation of Ag3PO4 and inhibited the photogenerated electron-holes pairs recombination of Ag3PO4 during the degradation of atenolol. Oxygen defects caused by the doping of Y3+ into Ag3PO4 were the centers of capture of photogenerated electrons to generate •O2 −, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-holes pairs and the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, there has been worldwide concern regarding the harmful effects of residual pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) in surface water and groundwater [1–5]. Atenolol, a typical non-antibiotic PPCP and a β1 receptor blocker, can retard the growth of embryos and poison aquatic organisms and biofilm [6]. Moreover, it can produce additional toxic chlorination products [7] by chlorination during the process of sewage disinfection. Therefore, it is imperative that efficient ways to eliminate atenolol from water be found, to ensure stability of the ecological environment.

The removal of some organic dyes and split water by photocatalytic degradation has been widely researched. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most commonly used photocatalytic semiconductor [8–10] due to its non-toxicity, stability, and affordability. However, its absorption of only ultraviolet (UV) light restricts its wider utilization in practice. Ag3PO4, with a quantum efficiency of over 90%, is regarded as an alternative photocatalyst, although its strong photocorrosion and poor photocatalytic characteristics make it far from ideal. Therefore, modifying combinations with other semiconductors [11–13] and doping of metal-ions [14,15] are frequently-used approaches to restrain the recombination of photogenerated carriers and thus, efficiently boost the photocatalytic activity and stability of Ag3PO4. Recently, a number of Ag3PO4-based photocatalysts have been reported [16], such as Ag3PO4/Ag/BiVO4 (040) [17], Ag3PO4/MIL-101/NiFe2O4 [18], and Ag3PO4/POM/GO [19]. The metal-ion doping modification of Ag3PO4 can cause light absorption redshift, introduce defects, and reduce the band gap of photocatalyst, thereby enhancing its photocatalytic activity [20–22].

In this study, we doped yttrium (Y) into Ag3PO4 and further prepared a composite photocatalyst using Y3+-doped Ag3PO4, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and BiVO4. The doping of Y3+ into Ag3PO4 can affect the band gap energy and enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs. CQDs are photocatalytic nanomaterials with unique up-conversion abilities as well as electron-migration properties [23]. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs could significantly increase the visible-light absorption and improve the photogenerated electron migration of composite photocatalysts. BiVO4, with a higher photocatalytic activity and visible-light absorption performance [24], could enhance the separation of photogenerated carriers of composite photocatalysts.
A Y3+-doped Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was prepared to facilitate the degradation of atenolol. The photocatalysis mechanism and possible degradation pathways of atenolol were identified.

All chemicals used were analytical reagents; these were used without any further purification. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW 24,000), yttrium nitrate hexahydrate (Y(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.5%), 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3, 99%), atenolol (C14H22N2O3, ≥98%), benzoquinone (BQ, 99%), and 5% nafion117 solution were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co. Citric acid, ethylene glycol (EG), anhydrous ethanol, t-butyl alcohol (TBA), and isopropyl alcohol were purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemicals Co. Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) were acquired from Guangzhou Chemicals Factory. Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Methanol (MA), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, 99.5%), and phosphoric acid were obtained from Kemiou Chemicals Co. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ag3PO4 and Y3+-doped Ag3PO4
ce:section.ce:para: 0.0097 mol AgNO3 and 0.0003 mol Y (NO3)3·6H2O were added to 100 mL deionized water with stirring. Afterwards, 100 mL 0.05 mol•L-1 Na2HPO4 was dripped into the mixed solution and stirred for 2 h without illumination. The precipitation was rinsed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. Finally, the as-synthesized Y3+-doped Ag3PO4 (denoted as Y-Ag3PO4) was dried at 65℃ for 12 h. The pristine Ag3PO4 was synthesized following the aforementioned approach without the addition of Y(NO3)3.

CQDs were prepared by thermal decomposition of citric acid. Here, 5.00 g citric acid was heated at 180℃ for 2 h in an oil bath pot. After turning orange, the colloid mixture was neutralized by dripping 1 mol•L-1 NaOH solution dropwise. The CQDs solution was adjusted to 500 mL with deionized water upon cooling and its concentration was determined to be 0.01 g•mL−1.

BiVO4 was prepared via the polyol method. In a 100 mL flask, 4.85 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 0.2 g PVP and 50 mL EG were added and magnetically stirred until dissolved completely. Then, 10 mmol NH4VO3 was added to the mixture and heated to 70℃. After dropwise, addition of 2 mL of deionized water, the mixture quickly turned turbid and yellow from the originally transparent and red brown. The mixture was heated to 100℃ and then, after 10 min stirring, it was maintained at reflux for 12 h. The precipitate was collected, rinsed, and dried in air for 3 h at 80℃. The dried yellow-green BiVO4 powder was transferred to a ceramic crucible and calcined in a tubular furnace at 550℃ for 6 h. Bright yellow BiVO4 powder was obtained after naturally cooling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs, Y-Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4
ce:section.ce:para: Y-Ag3PO4 and CQDs were separately added to two 50-mL isopropanol solutions and treated via ultrasound for 3 h. Subsequently, these two suspensions were mixed by vigorous stirring for 6 h. The achieved Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs was washed and dried in vacuum at 65℃ for 10 h. The preparation of Y-Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was achieved by a similar as that of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs. The Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was prepared as follows: first, Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs were produced and then, BiVO4 was added by stirring vigorously for 2 h. Subsequently, the Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and dried in vacuum at 60℃.

The crystallographic texture of all synthesized photocatalysts was measured by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima Ⅳ) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The morphological characteristics of the samples were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Gemini 500) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100HR). The binding energy of elements were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS SUPRA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were plotted on the FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two). UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600) with integrating spheres and reflectance standard material BaSO4 was applied to obtain the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of synthesized photocatalysts were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-4600). The active species were detected by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy (JEOL JES-FA200).

The photoelectric properties of synthesized photocatalysts were measured using an electrochemical measurement system (CHI660e, Chenhua Co.), with a 150 W Xe lamp having an UV cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm). The experiment was carried out using the traditional three-electrode system, comprising the reference electrode, counter electrode, and working electrode. The corresponding electrode materials were Ag/AgCl electrode (3 M KCl), Pt electrode, and FTO glass with prepared samples. The preparation procedure of the working electrode was as follows:: (i) the FTO conductive glass underwent ultrasonic cleaning pre-treatment using ultrapure water, acetone, and ethanol successively, and was dried in the oven at standby; (ii) 0.03 g prepared sample, 20 μL Nafion solution (5%), and 1 mL EG were added to 1 mL ethanol with ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min. A quantity of 25 μL of the above slurry was dripped on the FTO conductive glass using a pipette. After vacuum drying at 60℃ for 12 h, the required working electrode was achieved and 50 mL 0.1 mol•L-1 Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte solution.

The degradation of atenolol was used to assess the photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalyst. The photocatalytic reactor is equipped with a 250 W xenon lamp and a UV cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm). A quantity of 5 mg photocatalyst was added into the atenolol solution (10 mM, 50 mL) and stirred in the dark for 1 h. Then the xenon lamp was turned on, and a 1 mL suspension was sucked and filtered by a 0.22 μm filter organic membrane at given intervals. The residual concentration of atenolol was determined by high performance liquid chromatography (LC-20, Shimadzu equipped with a C18 column [250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5.0 μm] and a UV detector). The mobile phase comprised 87% methanol and 13% potassium dihydrogen phosphate (0.01 M, pH = 2.5). The column temperature was 35℃, detection wavelength was 229 nm and flow rate was 1 mL•min−1. The total organic carbon (TOC) was measured via TOC analyses (TOC-L, Shimadzu).

The degradation intermediates of atenolol were identified by ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC) coupled with Q-exactive orbitrap mass spectrometry (Q-exactive, Thermo Fisher). The ion source was HESI source and the data were collected in both negative and positive ionization mode at m/z 80–800.The conditions were set as follows: sheath spray voltage (2.8 kV), sheath gas flow rate (35 Arb), auxiliary gas flow rate (10 Arb), and source temperature (320 °C).

The XRD patterns of all prepared photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 1 . In this paper, Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 refers to the photocatalyst with an optimized composition proportion of 3% doping of Y3+, 0.7 wt% of CQDs to Y-Ag3PO4, and 5 wt% of BiVO4 to Y-Ag3PO4. Referring to JCPDF 06–0505 card, the lattice planes (110), (200), (210), (211), (310), (320) of Ag3PO4 match the body-centred cubic crystal structure of Ag3PO4 well. The partially enlarged image in Fig. 1a shows that the doping of Y3+ caused the strongest peak shift and peak splitting of Y-Ag3PO4 located at 2θ = 32–24° compared to that of Ag3PO4. The ESR peaks at g = 2.002 (Fig. 2 ) confirmed that there were more oxygen defects for Y-Ag3PO4 than Ag3PO4 [25]. The lattice planes of BiVO4 were also found in the XRD pattern of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4. Though the characteristic peak intensity of the used photocatalyst (Fig. 1b) was slightly weakened, the unchanged peak position indicated the strong photostability of the photocatalyst.

In Fig. 3 a, Y-Ag3PO4 was observed as a particle size of ~ 200–400 nm and rough spherical surface. BiVO4 exhibited large aggregates composing of small smooth particles (Fig. 3b). CQDs were approximately spherical quantum dots with a particle size of 1–10 nm (Fig. 3d). Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4 were combined through CQDs (Fig. 3c and f). Furthermore, the lattice spacing of 0.27 nm and 0.23 nm corresponded to the characteristic lattice plane of Y-Ag3PO4 and CQDs [26], respectively (Fig. 3e).

The elemental composition and chemical form of the photocatalyst were determined by means of XPS. According to the full spectrum of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 (Fig. 4 a), it consisted of Ag, P, C, O, Y, Bi, and V. These peaks at 374.0 eV and 367.9 eV in Fig. 4b represented the existence of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 respectively, indicating that there were monovalent silver ions in the photocatalyst [27]. The binding energy of P5+ in Y-Ag3PO4 was at 132.3 eV in Fig. 4c [28]. In Fig. 4d, the strong peak of O1s at 530.68 eV originated from the lattice oxygen of Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4. The characteristic peak at 531.2 eV was attributed to the hydroxyl ions in the water molecules adsorbed by the photocatalyst [29]. Two peaks were noted at 516.26 eV and 524.13 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of V 2p1/2 and V 2p3/2 in BiVO4 [30]. The two peaks at 160.3 eV and 158.26 eV in Fig. 4e represented Y 3d3/2 and Y 3d5/2. The valence state of Y in the Y-containing photocatalysts was + 3 [31]. The binding energy of Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 was located at 164.3 eV and 159.0 eV. The valence state of Bi in Bi-containing photocatalysts was + 3 [32]. Three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.27 eV, and 289.03 eV in Fig. 4f corresponded to C-C/C = C, C-O and O-C = O respectively, which indicates that CQDs contain sp2 hybrid carbons [26], and that CQDs were combined with Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4.

The optical property of photocatalysts can be acquired by UV–vis DRS spectra. Fig. 6 a indicated that the absorption edge of all photocatalysts was no>520 nm. The absorption intensity of all the composite photocatalysts was stronger than that of Y-Ag3PO4 in the entire light region except that of Y-Ag3PO4/BiVO4 at the wavelength of 350–480 nm. A similar absorption intensity for Y-Ag3PO4 as that of Ag3PO4 indicated that the doping of Y3+ into Ag3PO4 had almost no effect on the light-harvesting of Ag3PO4. As expected, the absorption intensity of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was markedly increased throughout the visible light spectrum due to the up-conversion ability of CQDs by capturing longer wavelength light to broaden the optical absorption range. The band gap energy of photocatalyst was calculated by the following equation (Eqs.(1)) [36]:
(1)
( A h ν )
2
= h ν -
E g
where A is absorbance; h is Planck constant; ν is light frequency and Eg is the bandgap energy. The bandgap energy of Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4 was plotted to be 2.39 eV and 2.44 eV respectively (Fig. 6b).

The conduction band energy (ECB) and valence band energy (EVB) of photocatalyst can be determined by Mulliken electronegativity theory (Eqs. (2) and (3)) [37]:
(2)
E
CB
=
E
VB
-
E g
(3)
E
VB
= χ -
E e
+ 0.5
E g
where χ is the electronegativity of the semiconductor; 5.96 eV [38] for Y-Ag3PO4 and 6.04 eV [39] for BiVO4. Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (ca. 4.5 eV vs. NHE). Hence, the ECB and EVB of Y-Ag3PO4 were calculated to be 0.27 eV and 2.66 eV, while those of BiVO4 were 0.33 eV and 2.77 eV.

The photoluminescence spectra of the synthesized photocatalysts were illustrated in Fig. 7 . As shown in the spectra, the abscissa values of the main excitation peak were ~ 530 nm. In general, the lower recombination of photogenerated electron-holes can be reflected by the weaker intensity of the excitation peak [40]. It was noted that the excitation peak intensity of Y-Ag3PO4 was significantly weaker than that of pristine Ag3PO4, due to the defects caused by Y3+ doping. The defects led to the recombination reduction of the photogenerated electron-holes of Ag3PO4. The weakest excitation peak intensity of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 among the photocatalysts confirmed that the doping of Y3+ and the introduction of CQDs and BiVO4 can effectively repress the recombination of the photogenerated electron-holes, and accordingly lead to a dramatic enhancement of photocatalytic performance of Ag3PO4.

In the atenolol degradation experiment, atenolol was stable in the absence of the photocatalyst, while BiVO4 had almost no effect on the photocatalytic degradation of atenolol under visible light as shown in Fig. 8 a due to the serious recombination of photogenerated carriers. Y-Ag3PO4 exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than pristine Ag3PO4. The degradation efficiency of atenolol by Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 reached up to 90.9% in 6 h, compared to 64.7% by Ag3PO4. The photocatalytic activity improvement of Ag3PO4 might stem from the synergistic effect of Y3+ doping and the introduction of CQDs and BiVO4. The degradation of atenolol corresponds with the first-order kinetic model (Fig. 8b). The kinetic rate constants (k) can be calculated by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood equation [41]. The k values of Ag3PO4,Y-Ag3PO4, Y-Ag3PO4/BiVO4, Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs, and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 are 0.18 h−1, 0.29 h−1, 0.32 h−1, 0.40 h−1, and 0.50 h−1, respectively. The degradation rate of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 is ~ 2.8 times that of pristine Ag3PO4.

The photostability of photocatalysts can be evaluated by the cycle test of atenolol degradation. Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was more stable than Y-Ag3PO4 and could still degrade 86.1% of atenolol in the fourth cycle test (Fig. 8c). Similarly, the increased photostability of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 benefited from the introduction of BiVO4 and CQDs.

The active species were determined in order to explore the photocatalytic mechanism during the photodegradation of atenolol by Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 (Fig. 9 ). TBA, BQ, and EDTA-2Na were added to the photocatalytic system as scavengers of •OH, •O2 −, and h+, respectively [41]. The atenolol could be degraded up to 90.9% in 6 h without any scavengers. After EDTA-2Na and TBA were added, 9.9% and 75.5% of atenolol were degraded. The addition of BQ also slightly inhibited the degradation of atenolol (85.2% of atenolol degradation). These results corroborated that the species of h+, •OH, and •O2 − dominated the degradation process of atenolol.

The generated •OH and •O2 − during atenolol degradation were further identified by ESR. As shown in Fig. 10 , neither Y-Ag3PO4 nor Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 could generate •OH or •O2 − without visible light illumination. The signal intensity of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 enhanced with the light irradiation time and was stronger than that of Y-Ag3PO4 under visible light illumination for DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − [42,43]. The generation of more •OH and •O2 − via the synergistic effect of Y-Ag3PO4, CQDs, BiVO4 enhanced the degradation of atenolol. It is known that the more positive ECB of both Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4 than O2/•O2 −cannot generate •O2 − [44]. The generated •O2 − of Y-Ag3PO4 and Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 might originate from the reaction of the captured photogenerated electrons of Y-Ag3PO4 with oxygen defects and adsorbed oxygen.

The pathways and intermediates of atenolol degradation by Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 were analyzed by ultra UPLC-MS. According to the total ion chromatogram (TIC) and FTMS + p full mass spectra (Fig. S1), protonated atenolol was found at m/z 267. The detected signals at m/z 134, 152, 238, 254, 283, and 331 revealed the possible intermediates of atenolol degradation. The retention time of these intermediates was earlier than that of atenolol, indicating that most of the intermediates were more hydrophilic than atenolol. Among them, the intermediate at m/z 283 is greater than protonated atenolol by 16 more amu, which can be regarded as a homologue of monohydroxylated atenolol. Similarly, the intermediate at m/z 331 is tetrahydroxylated atenolol. According to a previous study [45], the addition of hydroxyl was most likely to occur on 2C, 3C, 5C, and 6C on the benzene ring of atenolol. The degradation pathways and intermediates of atenolol were proposed considering the retention time and m/z (Fig. 11 ). There were three possible pathways for atenolol degradation: (i) Pathway I was ipso-substitution due to the attack of •OH on the 1C of the benzene ring of atenolol. According to the O-dealkylation mechanism [46], the ether branched chain on the benzene ring was split to form two products at m/z 152 and 134, respectively; (ii) Upon the attack of •OH, acetamide branched chain in atenolol molecules was split [47] to generate the intermediate at m/z 238, in reaction to adsorbed oxygen, and then the intermediate at m/z 238 reacted with •OH to generate the intermediate at m/z 254; and (iii) Owing to the electrophilic addition reaction between atenolol and •OH, four isomers at m/z 238 [48] and the intermediate at m/z 299 were inferred, as mentioned earlier.

The mineralization ratio of atenolol degradation was evaluated by TOC (Fig. 12 ). 52.1% of atenolol was mineralized by Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 within 10 h. In contrast, the mineralization ratio of atenolol degradation by Y-Ag3PO4 was only 34.3%. The mineralization data further confirmed that Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 could generate more radicals than Y-Ag3PO4 within a certain time.

Photoelectrochemical analysis was applied to measure the generation and migration of photogenerated carriers. The photocurrent density could reflect the efficiency of separation and migration of photogenerated carriers. Fig. 13 a showed the photocurrent density of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4, BiVO4, and Y-Ag3PO4 at 20 s intervals under Xe lamp irradiation. The photocurrent density of BiVO4 was only ~ 0.05 μA/cm2, while that of Y-Ag3PO4 could reach ~ 0.7 μA/cm2 in the early stage. The photocurrent density of Y-Ag3PO4 gradually declined to ~ 0.5μA/cm2 after 320 s because of the photocorrosion of Y-Ag3PO4. In contrast, the photocurrent density of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 far exceeded the sum of Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4, twice that of Y-Ag3PO4, and showed almost no decay within 340 s. It can be concluded that the synergistic effect of Y-Ag3PO4, CQDs, and BiVO4 not only enhanced the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated carriers, but also effectively suppressed the photocorrosion. Simultaneously, the resistance of electron migration at the interface was measured by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). As is known, a smaller arc radius results in a lower carrier migration resistance [17]. The EIS Nyquist plots in Fig. 13b illustrate that Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 had a smaller impedance than Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4, indicating that the ternary photocatalyst exhibited a better electron migration capability between interfaces.

Following the above findings, a possible Z-scheme mechanism of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 was proposed, as shown in Fig. 14 . Both Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4 generated the photogenerated carriers under visible light illumination. CQDs could not only increase the visible-light absorption of Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 by capturing longer wavelength light to broaden the optical absorption range, but could also act as an electron mediator. The doping of Y3+ caused more oxygen defects as the capture centre of photogenerated carriers further inhibited the recombination of photogenerated e- and h+. The CB of Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4 could not generate •O2 − (their ECB is more positive than O2/•O2 −), and the generated •O2 − originated from the reaction of the captured photogenerated electrons of Y-Ag3PO4 with oxygen defects and adsorbed oxygen [49]. Thus the photogenerated electrons in the CB of Y-Ag3PO4 were not easily migrated to the CB of BiVO4. The photogenerated electrons in the CB of BiVO4 could be migrated to the VB of Y-Ag3PO4 through CQDs as an electron mediator and then combined with the holes of Y-Ag3PO4. Therefore, the VB of BiVO4 enriched a substantial number of holes. The holes, •OH and •O2 − could all degrade atenolol. Therefore, the Z-scheme photocatalyst could accelerate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and produce more holes, •OH and •O2 − to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composite photocatalyst.

Overall, a Z-scheme Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 exhibited the highest degradation efficiency to degrade atenolol compared to Y-Ag3PO4 and BiVO4. The significant increase in photocatalytic activity was attributed to the synergistic effect of Y-Ag3PO4, BiVO4 and CQDs. CQDs could not only significantly increase the visible-light absorption of Y-Ag3PO4, but could also act as electron mediators to rapidly migrate photogenerated electrons from CB of BiVO4. Simultaneously, oxygen defects caused by the doping of Y3+ into Ag3PO4 was the capture centre of photogenerated electrons to generate •O2 −, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-holes pairs and the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4. This study provided an effective approach to boost the photocatalytic activity and photostability of Ag3PO4, which could propel the further application of advanced oxidation technology in wastewater treatment.

Yifei Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jing Niu: Visualization. Xin Gao: Writing - review & editing. Yanlin Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are greatly thankful for the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51578249).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.147458.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
